signed_reward.formula: signed_reward = +1-(1-p)^2 if judge=1 else -p^2; -0.25 for unparseable

brier_loss.formula: brier = (p - y)^2; 0.25 for unparseable (equivalent to p=0.5)
brier_loss.note: Uses the same extracted probability and judge correctness as signed reward

B_their_retrieval.description: OpenForecaster's published 'prompt' field verbatim (their retrieval, loose protocol)

C_loose_our_retrieval.description: Our GDELT corpus via union retrieval, articles <= resolution_date

C_strict_our_retrieval.description: Our GDELT corpus via union retrieval, articles <= question_start_date (strict forecasting filter)

D_dayminus1.description: Our GDELT corpus, articles <= resolution_date - 1 day (tests whether the final 24h drives leakage)

D_dayminus1_deleaked.description: Counterfactual: dayminus1 with leaky chunks identified by GPT-4o and stripped; 45 EXPLICIT questions re-run, merged with unchanged 446 non-EXPLICIT results
D_dayminus1_deleaked.note: Of the 45 EXPLICIT questions, 35 were correct before chunk stripping and only 15 correct after (−20 swing). The 446 non-EXPLICIT are carried from the original dayminus1 run.

leakage_audit.method: Two-stage GPT-4o audit via OpenAI batch API. Stage 1: question-level bundle classification (491 questions, EXPLICIT/IMPLIED/NO). Stage 2: chunk-level classification (225 chunks for the 45 EXPLICIT questions, YES/NO per chunk).

counterfactual_delta_strict_to_deleaked.interpretation: Essentially all of the dayminus1 lift over strict (+5.5 pp) is attributable to articles that literally state the answer; stripping those brings accuracy within 1.4 pp of strict.

leakage_effect_same_corpus.interpretation: Articles published between question creation and resolution account for roughly half of apparent retrieval lift on this benchmark.

retrieval_vs_no_context_at_matched_proto.interpretation: Their retrieval improves accuracy but hurts mean reward (confidence inflation without correctness gain); our retrieval improves both.
